mechanism: Participants generate narratives describing past and future events. Narratives are transcribed and segmented into atomic details classified as internal (episodic) or external. Counts of each detail type are computed per participant and per temporal direction (past vs. future). Correlations between counts are calculated across temporal conditions, and linear relationships between internal detail counts and relational memory test scores are estimated.
domain: cognitive aging, episodic memory, future simulation

mechanism: Visual search task presents target and distractor stimuli at two presentation durations. Eye gaze is recorded and spatially localized. Binary outcomes (target fixation yes/no) and continuous outcome (fixation duration) are measured separately by stimulus viewpoint category (prototypical vs. nonprototypical). Proportions of first fixations on target and mean fixation durations are compared across conditions.

Participants are randomly assigned to read passages inducing belief in determinism or neutral control. Binary outcome (cheating yes/no or payment overage) is recorded. Belief in free will is measured via self-report scale. Logistic regression or proportional analysis is applied to test direct effects of message type on cheating, and mediation analysis decomposes the effect through belief measure.
domain: moral behavior, free will belief, decision-making

mechanism: Rapid serial visual presentation of stimuli with two designated targets separated by variable stimulus onset asynchronies. Binary outcome (target detection yes/no) recorded per target. Distractor intrusion frequencies (counts of misidentified distractors) measured at each stimulus onset asynchrony. Intrusion counts modeled as functions of onset asynchrony to decompose suppression, delay, and diffusion effects across time points.

Static visual images containing smooth convex surfaces with and without extremal edges are presented. Participants report perceived relative depth and figure-ground assignment (categorical ordinal: which surface closer). Proportions of responses per edge type are computed. Comparison of response proportions across edge conditions (extremal vs. non-extremal) tests superiority of extremal edges as depth cues.

Pairs of acoustic chords (frequency combinations) are presented with variable interstimulus intervals. A frequency change in one tone is introduced in the second chord (location unpredictable). Participant response (same/different judgment or ordinal comparison: higher/lower) recorded as binary correct/incorrect. Accuracy proportions computed per complexity level (number of tones) and interstimulus interval. Linear or logistic regression models the effect of complexity on accuracy independent of interval.

Participants read object words (e.g., head, foot) followed by visual target presentation at variable screen locations. Binary outcome recorded (target identification correct/incorrect). Reaction time or accuracy measured per target location relative to object's typical location (high vs. low on body). Comparison of accuracy/reaction time when target location matches vs. mismatches object location tests attentional orientation effect.

Participants classified by motivational tendency (external motivation to avoid prejudice: low vs. high). Dot-probe paradigm presents pairs of faces (White and Black male) with facial expressions (neutral or happy) for brief duration. Binary outcome: first fixation location (toward Black or White face). Response latencies measured. Proportions of fixations toward neutral Black faces computed per duration (30 ms vs. 450 ms) and motivation group. Analysis of variance or logistic regression tests interaction of motivation, race, expression, and duration on fixation patterns.

Participants perform rapid-response task; errors and correct responses are recorded. Scalp electroencephalography measures voltage deflections in event-related potentials time-locked to response, with magnitude of error-related negativity (continuous measure) extracted. Separately, acoustic startle stimulus presented after errors and correct responses; electromyographic response amplitude (continuous) measured. Pearson correlation computed between error-related negativity magnitude and post-error startle amplitude, and t-test compares startle magnitude across error vs. correct conditions.

Participants classified as chronically lonely or induced into loneliness via social exclusion task. Self-report measures quantify anthropomorphism of nonhuman agents (animals, objects) and belief in humanized religious agents. Loneliness (continuous measure) is the predictor. Linear regression or correlation analysis tests whether variation in loneliness predicts variation in anthropomorphism and religious belief, with hypothesized mediation through affective state tested via path analysis.

Participants view numerical anchors that are either precise (e.g., 643) or rounded (e.g., 600) and then generate estimates of a target quantity (continuous outcome). Magnitude of adjustment away from anchor (difference between estimate and anchor value) is computed per participant per condition. Mean adjustment magnitude compared between precise and rounded anchor conditions via t-test or analysis of variance.

Female participants rate attractiveness of male targets described as single or attached. Attractiveness rating (continuous ordinal scale) is the outcome. Fertility level is inferred from menstrual cycle timing (categorical grouping). Analysis of variance or mixed-effects regression tests main effects of partner status and interaction with fertility phase on attractiveness ratings, stratified by male phenotype (masculine vs. non-masculine).

During functional magnetic resonance imaging, participants evaluate stimuli (famous people names) along three conditions: positive valence, negative valence, or overall mixed valence. Blood oxygen level-dependent signal (continuous neuroimaging measure) is recorded in amygdala region of interest. Magnitude of amygdala activation per stimulus type (positive vs. negative names) and condition is extracted. Analysis of variance or mixed-effects regression tests interaction of stimulus valence with evaluation goal on amygdala activation magnitude.

Stimuli are presented with manipulated fluency (visual clarity, priming strength, linguistic ease). Participants make categorical judgments of abstraction level (concrete vs. abstract) or distance rating (continuous scale). Fluency is varied as categorical factor. Proportion of abstract judgments or mean distance rating is predicted by fluency status (high vs. low) via logistic or linear regression. Additional measurements use visual degradation or priming delay to manipulate fluency orthogonally.

Participants study word lists under different encoding contexts: rating for survival relevance, rating for imagery/pleasantness/self-reference, generation, intention-to-learn, or contextual scenario relevance. Memory is tested via free recall (count of correctly recalled words) or recognition (binary correct/incorrect). Proportion or count of recalled/recognized items computed per encoding condition. Analysis of variance tests main effect of encoding condition on retention, with planned contrasts comparing survival condition to other conditions.

Child is positioned so experimenter can see one of two objects but not the other, or vice versa. Experimenter looks at visible object and produces novel word in statement (there) or question (where) frame. Child responds by pointing, selecting target object (binary correct/incorrect or ordinal preference ranking). Accuracy or proportion of correct responses computed per age group, per trial type (there vs. where), and per knowledge-state condition (mutual visibility vs. asymmetric visibility). Mixed-effects logistic regression or analysis of variance models trial type effects across ages.
domain: developmental psychology, language learning, theory of mind

mechanism: Participant views colored object and produces name (color or object name, counterbalanced). Response phonological properties (phoneme inventory) are recorded. Phonological similarity between object name and color name is computed (binary or continuous distance metric). Reaction time to naming and error rate (binary) are measured. Analysis of variance or regression models main effect of phonological similarity and interaction with naming task (color vs. object) on latency and accuracy, stratified by language (English vs. French) in which word-order conventions differ.

Participants learn behaviors of two individuals from different groups (one positive, one negative per group). Implicit attitude is measured via reaction-time task (continuous latency or error rate); explicit attitude via self-report (continuous scale). New individuals from same groups are introduced. Implicit and explicit attitudes toward originals are measured again immediately and at follow-up days later. Implicit attitude score and explicit attitude score computed per individual, per group, and per time point. Generalization quantified as correlation or regression slope predicting new individual attitude from learned individual attitude, tested at immediate and delayed timepoints.
domain: social psychology, attitude generalization, implicit bias

mechanism: Participants first perform self-control task or control task, then consume beverage (sugar vs. sugar substitute, randomized). Participants make binary choice in attraction effect paradigm (two options with decoy present vs. absent). Choice outcome (selection of option A vs. B) recorded as binary. Proportion selecting target option computed per condition. Logistic regression or analysis of variance tests main effect of self-control depletion and interaction with glucose (sugar vs. substitute) on attraction effect magnitude (binary choice bias).

Participants are primed with achievement goal (nonconscious) or control priming, then perform implicit learning task on novel structured environment (e.g., grammar learning, sequence prediction). Learning is measured as classification accuracy (binary correct/incorrect) or continuous performance metric (e.g., reaction time decrease over trials). Accuracy or learning trajectory computed per condition. Analysis of variance or regression compares learning rate between primed and control conditions, tested on completely novel environment structure.
domain: cognitive psychology, implicit learning, goal priming
